title: Trump Weighs Iran Deal as Global Institutions Warn of Fuel Crisis
subhead: The IMF, World Bank, and IEA issued a joint warning that prolonged Hormuz closure risks global fuel shortages this summer, while Trump convened a Situation Room meeting on a tentative 60-day ceasefire extension that would reopen the strait and require Iran to destroy its 60%-enriched uranium stockpile.

sections.heading: Trump's Decision Point: Ceasefire Extension and Uranium Disposal
sections.body:
President Donald Trump held a roughly two-hour Situation Room meeting with national security advisers on Friday but has not yet decided whether to approve a tentative deal with Iran, a senior administration official told the Associated Press. Trump said he was seeking a 'final determination' and would only sign an agreement that 'satisfies his redlines' on curbing Iran's nuclear ambitions.

The proposed memorandum, as outlined by U.S. officials, would extend the fragile ceasefire by 60 days, require Iran to remove all mines from the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days, and prohibit Tehran from imposing tolls on the waterway. In return, the U.S. would gradually lift its blockade on Iranian ports and relax sanctions to allow more Iranian oil sales.

Trump posted on social media that 'Iran must agree that they will never have a Nuclear Weapon or Bomb' and that the strait must be reopened for international navigation. The president also demanded that Iran's 440.9 kilograms of uranium enriched to 60% purity — a short technical step from weapons-grade — be unearthed from damaged nuclear sites and 'DESTROYED,' according to his post.

Iranian officials struck a cautious tone. Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei told state broadcaster IRIB that the agreement 'has not been finalized yet' and that Iranian officials are 'focused on the end of war and are not discussing the details of the nuclear plan at this point.' Parliament national security commission head Ebrahim Azizi posted that Iran 'sets the terms: cash for cash, credit for credit, nothing for nothing.'

Iran's chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf wrote on X: 'No step will be taken before the other side acts. We do not gain concessions through talks, but through missiles,' underscoring deep distrust after the U.S. and Israel twice attacked Iran during nuclear negotiations over the past year.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal would mark the first major de-escalation in the U.S.-Iran conflict since February, potentially reopening the world's most critical oil transit chokepoint — but hardline rhetoric on both sides suggests implementation could falter before it begins.

sections.heading: IMF, World Bank, IEA Heads Warn of Global Fuel Shortages
sections.body:
The heads of the International Monetary Fund, World Bank, and International Energy Agency issued a joint warning that global fuel supplies are at risk if the Strait of Hormuz crisis persists into summer, according to NDTV Profit. The coordinated statement from three major global institutions underscores escalating concern about energy security as the blockade enters its third month.

Before the conflict, roughly 35% of global crude oil exports, 20% of liquefied natural gas exports, and up to 30% of fertilizer exports transited through Hormuz, according to CSIS analysis. The blockade has severely disrupted global supply chains just as planting seasons advance across both hemispheres.

CSIS analysis notes that daily transits through the strait remain 'well below pre-conflict levels.' While some vessels may be operating 'dark' with AIS transponders switched off, 'commercial traffic through the strait largely has not recovered.' Of the 187 vessels that have successfully transited since March 4, over half are operated by shipping companies in just four countries — with China at the top of that list, given Beijing's pressure on Tehran to protect Chinese shipping.
sections.whyItMatters: The unprecedented joint warning from the IMF, World Bank, and IEA signals that the institutional guardrails of the global economy are now publicly alarmed about energy disruption — a diplomatic signal that pressure is mounting for resolution before peak summer demand.

sections.heading: Food Crisis Cascade: Fertilizer Prices Surge 20-60%
sections.body:
The Hormuz crisis is triggering a cascading shock to global agrifood systems, according to World Economic Forum analysis citing FAO Chief Economist Máximo Torero. Before the conflict, up to 30% of global fertilizer exports — along with sulfur essential for phosphate fertilizer production — transited through Hormuz.

Farmers now face urea fertilizer price increases of 20% to 60% on top of rising fuel, transport, and irrigation costs. The World Economic Forum warns the greatest risk is not immediate food shortages but 'cascading shocks' that reduce future food production: energy-price spikes and logistics disruptions, followed by fertilizer shortages, then lower yields, with delayed effects leading to higher food prices months later.

The FAO's Torero writes that 'cascading shocks mean decision-makers must act now to stave off the worst possible consequences.' He calls for targeted programs for vulnerable households, avoiding export restrictions, and reactivating the Food Import Financing Facility and IMF Food Shock Window.
sections.whyItMatters: The fertilizer-to-food transmission mechanism means the real food security impact will hit 6-12 months from now — creating a delayed crisis that policymakers can still mitigate but only if they act before the next planting cycles.

sections.heading: U.S. Blocks Sixth Ship in Iranian Port Blockade
sections.body:
The U.S. military stopped a sixth vessel — the Gambia-flagged bulk carrier Lian Star — as part of its enforcement of the blockade on Iranian ports, AP News reported. The interception is part of an ongoing campaign to maintain pressure on Tehran amid fragile ceasefire negotiations.

The U.S. has maintained a naval presence in the region since the conflict began, conducting regular interdictions of vessels suspected of violating sanctions or carrying contraband. The latest interception comes as commercial shipping through Hormuz remains constrained, with Iran's self-declared 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority' condemning new U.S. sanctions but claiming they demonstrate the authority's 'positive performance' in managing limited traffic.
sections.whyItMatters: Each interdiction reinforces the blockade architecture while creating new points of friction — the Lian Star interception signals that military pressure continues alongside diplomatic negotiations.

sections.heading: AUKUS Develops Underwater Drones for Cable Protection
sections.body:
The AUKUS partnership — Australia, the United Kingdom, and the United States — is developing underwater drone technology to protect undersea cables, the BBC reported. The UK is contributing £150 million to the effort, with systems expected to be ready 'by next year.'

The initiative responds to growing concerns about critical infrastructure vulnerability. Undersea cables carry approximately 95% of international data traffic, and recent years have seen increased attention to their security amid suspected sabotage incidents in the Baltic and elsewhere.

The AUKUS announcement comes the same week that 17 nations launched a pact to protect undersea cables, notably with the United States and China absent from the agreement, according to the South China Morning Post. The pact reflects growing multilateral attention to infrastructure security even as geopolitical rivalries complicate collective action.
sections.whyItMatters: Undersea cable protection is emerging as a new domain of infrastructure competition — with AUKUS moving operationally while broader multilateral efforts proceed without the two powers most often suspected of interference.

sections.heading: Russia-China Power of Siberia 2: Agreement on Route, Not Price
sections.body:
Russia and China reached an 'understanding' on the Power of Siberia 2 (POS-2) pipeline during this week's summit between Presidents Vladimir Putin and Xi Jinping, Al Jazeera reported. The proposed 2,600km pipeline would carry 50 billion cubic metres of natural gas per year from western Siberia through Mongolia to China — equivalent to about 525 terawatt-hours of energy, nearly twice the UK's annual electricity consumption.

Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said there is 'agreement on the route and on how the project will be built. Some details still need to be finalised' — but acknowledged there is no timetable for construction.

Analysts told Al Jazeera the holdup remains commercial terms, specifically price. Russia urgently needs revenue to replace lost European gas exports, but China has the upper hand in negotiations. 'Beijing has reportedly pushed for prices linked to Russia's highly subsidised domestic gas rates. Moscow wants terms closer to POS-1,' said Seb Kennedy, CEO of Energy Flux. 'Every dollar shaved off the netback is a direct hit to Russian fiscal revenue.'

Kpler analyst Go Katayama noted that POS-2 would 'structurally reduce China's future LNG import requirements' by replacing marginal spot LNG demand with pipeline gas, potentially easing competition for Atlantic Basin cargoes. The project may take a decade from start of construction to full capacity, according to Oxford Institute for Energy Studies senior research fellow Jack Sharples.
sections.whyItMatters: The pipeline would lock in Russia's energy pivot to China for decades — but Beijing's leverage means Moscow may accept deeply discounted terms, accelerating a buyer's market for Russian gas.

sections.heading: Oil Prices: Brent at $91.70, Up 46% Year-on-Year
sections.body:
Brent crude settled at $91.70 per barrel on May 29, 2026 — down 1.08% on the day and down 16.94% over the month, but up 46.07% year-on-year, according to Trading Economics data. The price reflects continued market adjustment to Hormuz disruption while factoring in ceasefire negotiation developments.

European natural gas prices have risen sharply since the crisis began, with TTF gas at €46.04/MWh on May 29, up 35.98% year-on-year. HSBC estimates European natural gas prices will remain 40% higher than previously projected through 2026 and stay elevated through 2027 due to war-related disruption.

The CSIS analysis notes the United States remains 'relatively insulated from rising natural gas prices' due to abundant domestic production, leaving European and Asian allies more exposed to ongoing disruption.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil markets are pricing in de-escalation hopes but gas markets remain elevated — a divergence that reflects the structural reconfiguration of global energy trade away from chokepoint-dependent routes.

bottomLine: Trump's deliberations on the Iran ceasefire extension represent the day's key decision point — a deal would reopen Hormuz and pause nuclear tensions, but hardliner opposition in Tehran and implementation hurdles remain significant. The coordinated IMF-World Bank-IEA warning on fuel shortages and the WEF analysis on cascading fertilizer-to-food shocks signal that the institutional architecture of the global economy is now mobilizing around energy security concerns. Meanwhile, infrastructure competition continues: AUKUS advances underwater drone capabilities, 17 nations launch a cable protection pact without U.S. or Chinese participation, and Russia and China agree on pipeline routing while disagreeing on price. Oil prices remain elevated year-on-year despite daily fluctuations, and European gas prices stay structurally higher than pre-crisis norms.
sources: AP News, NDTV Profit, BBC, South China Morning Post, Al Jazeera, World Economic Forum, CSIS, Trading Economics, IEA, Oxford Institute for Energy Studies, Kpler, HSBC
